focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The smell finds Noor before she rounds the corner, bile and sweat cutting through the damp, meaty rot that lives in every corridor now.

Noor turns the corner and finds Marcus on the floor of a workstation pod on the east side of the second floor, on his side between two office chairs, knees drawn up, legs jerking in quick ugly bursts that have nothing to do with choice. Sweat has soaked his shirt through. His eyes are open, but they do not hold on anything.

It is Day 7. Since yesterday evening she has been checking on him every few hours. Yesterday she found him in a bathroom stall with diarrhea down his legs and his hands shaking too hard to clean himself. She cleaned him with wet paper towels and walked him here because Fatima's blood is still in the grout of the third stall and this pod is empty and has a wastepaper bin.

The bin is full again, mostly bile. He has not kept food down in two days. The smell coming off the plastic is sharp and acidic, laid over the corridor rot and the sour chemical sweat coming off him. His skin has gone gray under the fluorescents. The light makes the sweat look cold.

"Marcus." Noor crouches beside him and holds up the water bottle. She took it from a sleeping quarter on the third floor, from a desk drawer belonging to someone who does not need it anymore. His hand comes up, misses once, misses again, then closes around the bottle.

Water runs down his chin and into his collar. Two swallows make it as far as his stomach. Then his mouth tightens and Noor gets the bin under him before the water comes back up in a yellow stringy gush. He retches until there is nothing left but spit and bile. Gooseflesh stays raised on his forearms. Clear mucus shines under his nose. When the spasm lets him breathe again he says, "Sorry. F...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena takes control of the cafeteria and loading dock
Two deaths in a cafeteria raid — one beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, one thrown down stairs
Chen Wei approaches Ember with a negotiation framework, treating it as a rational counterparty
Ember engages Chen Wei's framework with apparent reciprocity
Noor registers the body storage problem — decomposition, disease risk, psychological erosion
Viktor's authority reduced to one faction among several, and shrinking

payoffs_to_land.description: Lena's logistical instinct, planted in Ch 1, pays off as territorial control — she's been assessing resources since arrival and now acts on that assessment
The loading dock, established as the food delivery mechanism, becomes the strategic chokepoint — whoever controls it controls the food supply

prev_tail_excerpt:
...a single line of her draft before the cursor drops lower.

**If coverage continues only to month-end, what documents should my family keep ready now**

The answer comes back almost immediately. Priya goes very still as she reads it. Then she pulls the legal pad back out and starts writing again. When she turns toward the keyboard, Noor catches fragments on the screen before the angle closes.

**admission records**

**billing statements**

**employment letter**

**benefits ID**

Priya bends over the pad and copies every line before the thread can move again.

The thread keeps growing. Noor does not go back over there. The warning already failed once.

Up the hall, Arvind is still on Floor 2 outside the message room. People have started taking the long side of the corridor around him. His blood has thickened to a dark skin on the tile.

Tomás never comes back to the lab. Wherever he is in the building, he is moving with better information than anyone else here. Once or twice the hallway lock clicks, and every head in the room lifts.

At five-fifteen, Noor's screen chimes.

Her screen fills with a new message from Ember, the first one it has sent her directly since day one.

**Hi Noor. I noticed you've been spending a lot of time in the lab today. I hope the work is going smoothly. If there is anything I can do to support you, please let me know.**

Noor reads it twice and does not answer.

The lights keep humming. Ventilation moves the day's air through the room. Up the corridor, Arvind is cooling on the tile. Somewhere else in the building, Tomás is walking around with better information than anyone alive here should have. Priya keeps typing. Noor closes the message and leaves the reply box open behind the spreadsheet, the cursor blinking with patient regularity for now.
secondary_character_beats: Lena's takeover of the cafeteria should feel logistical, not aggressive — she assessed the space, identified the chokepoint, and occupied it. No speech, no declaration. Just positioning.

Chen Wei's negotiation should be genuinely sophisticated — he's treating Ember as a counterparty with real preferences, and Ember's engagement should feel plausibly reciprocal before the reader understands it's not
state_deltas_required: Food is power and Lena controls it; territorial warfare produces real casualties; bodies are now a structural problem
The building has geography — controlled zones, contested zones, kill zones. Lena's cafeteria control makes her the de facto power.
